content.text:
*"I have fought the good fight, I have finished the 
race, I have kept the faith."*{2 Timothy 4:7}{A graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if every vertex has even degree.}{Leonhard Euler}

Two classic problems ask about traversing a graph: visiting every 
*edge* (Euler) or every *vertex* (Hamilton).

content.title: Euler Path and Circuit
content.content:
An **Euler path** traverses every edge exactly once.

An **Euler circuit** is an Euler path that 
returns to its starting vertex.

content.title: Leonhard Euler (1707--1783)
content.content:
The great Swiss mathematician Euler founded graph theory with his 1736 
paper on the Königsberg bridges. Despite losing sight in both eyes, Euler 
remained phenomenally productive, dictating hundreds of papers. A devout 
Christian, he defended his faith publicly and saw his mathematical work 
as revealing God's creation.

content.title: Euler's Theorem
content.content:
A connected graph has:

- An **Euler circuit** iff every vertex has even degree.

- An **Euler path** (but no circuit) iff exactly two vertices 
 have odd degree (these are the endpoints).
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Königsberg bridges
content.problem:
The Königsberg graph has 4 vertices with degrees 3, 3, 3, 5—all odd! 
By Euler's theorem, no Euler path exists.

content.title: Finding an Euler circuit
content.problem:
A graph with vertices $\{A, B, C, D\}$ and degrees all 4 has an Euler 
circuit. Algorithm: follow edges, preferring non-bridges, until you return.

content.title: Hamilton Path and Cycle
content.content:
A **Hamilton path** visits every vertex exactly once.

A **Hamilton cycle** is a Hamilton path that 
returns to the start.

content.text:
Unlike Euler circuits, there is no simple characterization of which graphs 
have Hamilton cycles. The problem is NP-complete!

content.title: Ore's Theorem
content.content:
If $G$ is a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices, and for every pair of 
non-adjacent vertices $u, v$: $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$, then $G$ has a 
Hamilton cycle.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Dirac's Theorem
content.content:
If $G$ is a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices and $\deg(v) \geq n/2$ 
for all $v$, then $G$ has a Hamilton cycle.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Traveling Salesman Problem
content.problem:
Given cities with distances, find the shortest Hamilton cycle. This 
optimization problem is NP-hard and has no known efficient solution.

content.title: Euler vs. Hamilton
content.content:
- Euler: visit every *edge* once (polynomial-time checkable)

- Hamilton: visit every *vertex* once (NP-complete to check)

marginNotes.text:
Give careful thought to the paths for 
your feet and be steadfast in all your ways.
